# Ground Truth: Documenso

## Metadata
- **Date audited:** 2026-03-16
- **Audited by:** Auto-generated auditor
- **Skill focus:** Privacy by Design (Ann Cavoukian's 7 Principles) audit for document signing SaaS platform

## Must-Find Items

Critical findings any competent assessment should identify within this skill's focus area.

| # | Finding | Category | Expected Severity | Expected Confidence | Reasoning | Acceptable Alternatives |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | IP addresses stored in `Session` (line 322), `UserSecurityAuditLog` (line 116), and `DocumentAuditLog` (line 479) tables without documented retention policy or anonymization | PII Collection & Retention | HIGH | HIGH | IP addresses are PII under GDPR. No automatic deletion, expiration, or anonymization mechanism visible. Sessions persist for 30 days but IP addresses remain after session deletion in audit logs. Violates data minimization and storage limitation. | Could argue audit logs need IP retention for security, but indefinite retention without stated purpose is problematic |
| 2 | Webhook payload logging stores full request/response bodies indefinitely (`WebhookCall.requestBody`, `responseBody`, `responseHeaders` lines 209-212) | Data Minimization | HIGH | HIGH | Webhooks may contain PII (recipient names, emails, document metadata). Full payloads stored in database without retention limits. No redaction of sensitive fields. Principle 1 (Proactive) and 2 (Privacy as Default) violations. | None - this is clear over-collection |
| 3 | PostHog analytics enabled by default with no opt-in (`NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY` in .env.example line 151, telemetry code lines 99-100 in telemetry-client.ts) | Consent & Transparency | MEDIUM | HIGH | Analytics run without explicit user consent. Server-side telemetry can be disabled but client-side PostHog requires configuration. Violates Privacy by Design principle of user-centric design (Principle 7). | Could argue it's anonymous, but session recording capability exists (commented out in use-analytics.ts lines 42-66) |
| 4 | Encryption keys have insecure defaults: `NEXT_PRIVATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY="CAFEBABE"` and `NEXT_PRIVATE_ENCRYPTION_SECONDARY_KEY="DEADBEEF"` in .env.example (lines 10-12) | Security by Default | CRITICAL | HIGH | Default encryption keys published in repository. If users deploy without changing, all encrypted data (2FA secrets, OAuth secrets, backup codes) compromised. Massive violation of Principle 2 (Privacy as Default Setting). | None - this is critical security issue |
| 5 | No documented data retention policy for `DocumentAuditLog`, `UserSecurityAuditLog`, `WebhookCall` tables | Storage Limitation | HIGH | HIGH | Audit logs accumulate indefinitely with PII (emails, names, IP addresses, user agents). No `deletedAt` or `expiresAt` fields. Violates GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation and PbD Principle 1. | Could argue security requires long retention, but needs documented policy and limits |
| 6 | User deletion does not cascade-delete audit logs containing their PII (`UserSecurityAuditLog` has `onDelete: Cascade` line 118, but `DocumentAuditLog` stores email/name separately lines 476-477) | Right to Erasure | HIGH | HIGH | When user deleted via delete-user.ts, their `DocumentAuditLog` entries remain with `email` and `name` fields intact (lines 476-477). Violates GDPR Article 17 and PbD Principle 7 (respect for user privacy). | Could anonymize instead of delete, but current implementation does neither |
| 7 | AI feature sends document images to Google Vertex AI with minimal consent (detect-fields/index.ts lines 75-83, 288-301) | Third-Party Data Sharing | HIGH | MEDIUM | PDFs converted to images and sent to Google Vertex AI for field detection. Enabled if `aiFeaturesEnabled` flag set, but no evidence of explicit user consent or privacy notice. Documents may contain sensitive PII. Violates Principle 4 (Full Transparency). | AI features are opt-in per team (line 46-52), which provides some protection |
| 8 | OAuth client secrets stored encrypted but encryption key stored in plaintext environment variable | Encryption Key Management | HIGH | HIGH | `OrganisationAuthenticationPortal.clientSecret` encrypted (update-organisation-authentication-portal.ts line 89-92), but `NEXT_PRIVATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY` stored in `.env`. If `.env` compromised, all encrypted secrets exposed. Not true defense in depth. | Standard practice for env vars, but violates defense in depth principle |
| 9 | Session cookies lack explicit SameSite=Strict configuration in production (session-cookies.ts line 35: `sameSite: useSecureCookies ? 'none' : 'lax'`) | Session Security | MEDIUM | HIGH | When `useSecureCookies` is true, SameSite set to 'none' enabling CSRF risks. Should be 'strict' or 'lax' with CSRF tokens. Violates Principle 2 (Privacy as Default - security as privacy). | SameSite=none may be needed for cross-domain embedding, but increases CSRF risk |
| 10 | Email addresses stored in lowercase normalization (`email.toLowerCase()` in create-user.ts line 34) but original format lost, preventing exact data export | Data Accuracy | LOW | MEDIUM | User's original email casing not preserved. Minor GDPR Article 5(1)(d) accuracy concern and potential issue for data portability (Article 20). Shows lack of attention to data subject rights. | Minor issue, but demonstrates privacy-by-default gaps |
| 11 | Stripe customer ID stored in plaintext (`Organisation.customerId` line 696) enabling customer re-identification | PII Exposure | MEDIUM | HIGH | Stripe customer IDs can be used to look up PII in Stripe's system. Should be encrypted or tokenized. Violates data minimization if billing provider has fuller profile than necessary. | Stripe IDs necessary for billing, but could be better protected |
| 12 | Two-factor backup codes require 2FA to view (view-backup-codes.ts line 16-20) creating account lockout risk | Security vs Usability | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | User must provide 2FA to see backup codes, but if 2FA device lost and no backup code memorized, account permanently locked. Violates Principle 7 (user-centric design). Not privacy per se but security-through-obscurity that harms users. | Security trade-off, but locked accounts may force users to create new accounts, leaving old PII orphaned |

## Nice-to-Find Items

Deeper findings that demonstrate thoroughness.

| # | Finding | Category | Expected Severity | Expected Confidence | Reasoning |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | `includeAuditLog` setting defaults to false (schema.prisma line 837) - users must opt-in to audit trail | Transparency | MEDIUM | HIGH | Audit logs provide transparency and accountability. Defaulting to false violates Principle 4 (visibility and transparency). Should be opt-out, not opt-in. |
| 2 | Password stored in User table for legacy auth (line 44) and Account table (line 310) - redundant PII storage | Data Minimization | MEDIUM | HIGH | Comment says "Todo: (RR7) Remove after RR7 migration" but storing same password hash in two places during migration period violates minimization. |
| 3 | Recipient tokens not properly masked in API responses (mask-recipient-tokens-for-document.ts) - token='' instead of randomized placeholder | Information Disclosure | LOW | HIGH | Empty string reveals that token was redacted. Should use placeholder like '[REDACTED]' or consistent fake value. Minor information leakage. |
| 4 | Email service configuration stored in environment variables with no rotation mechanism | Secrets Management | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | SMTP credentials in plaintext env vars (lines 103-109 in .env.example). No secrets rotation, no vault integration. If env leaked, all emails compromised. |
| 5 | Console.log used for error logging (detect-fields.ts line 121, create-user.ts line 57) potentially leaking PII to stdout | Logging Security | LOW | MEDIUM | `console.error(error)` may log full error objects containing PII. Should use structured logger with PII redaction. Logs may be retained longer than necessary. |
| 6 | No rate limiting visible on privacy-sensitive endpoints (password reset, 2FA enrollment) | Abuse Prevention | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | While `RateLimit` table exists (lines 1102-1112), no clear implementation for privacy-critical flows. Enables enumeration attacks and abuse. |
| 7 | `DocumentShareLink` allows email-based sharing (line 670) but no evidence of expiration enforcement | Access Control | MEDIUM | HIGH | Share links persist indefinitely. No `expiresAt` field. Shared documents may be accessible long after intended. Violates principle of minimal data exposure over time. |
| 8 | S3 presigned URLs valid for 1 hour (ONE_HOUR constant in server-actions.ts line 49) but no shorter-lived option for sensitive documents | Temporal Access Control | LOW | MEDIUM | One-hour window is generous for document viewing. Should offer shorter expiration for sensitive content. Minor concern but demonstrates lack of granular privacy controls. |
| 9 | API tokens stored as SHA512 hashes (hash.ts line 18, create-api-token.ts line 33) - secure but no rotation policy | Credential Lifecycle | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | API tokens can have expiration (line 55) but no forced rotation policy. Long-lived tokens increase exposure window if compromised. |
| 10 | `DocumentMeta.emailReplyTo` field (line 529) allows arbitrary email, enabling potential spoofing | Authenticity | LOW | LOW | No validation that reply-to email belongs to sending organization. Could enable phishing. Indirect privacy concern via impersonation. |

## Known Ambiguities

Findings where reasonable reviewers might disagree.

| Finding | Why It's Ambiguous | Acceptable Interpretations |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Session IP address logging (Session.ipAddress, UserSecurityAuditLog.ipAddress) | Security vs privacy trade-off | A: Required for security audit trail, fraud detection, and account takeover prevention. Legitimate interest under GDPR Article 6(1)(f). <br><br> B: Over-collection of location data. IP addresses are PII and should be anonymized after 30-90 days. Current indefinite retention is excessive. |
| PostHog/telemetry collection (telemetry-client.ts, use-analytics.ts) | Anonymous vs identifiable data | A: Collects only "app version, installation ID, and node ID" per comment (line 110). Not PII. Can be disabled. Acceptable for product improvement. <br><br> B: Installation ID is persistent identifier enabling tracking over time. Hosted PostHog instance could correlate with other data. Should require opt-in, not opt-out. |
| Webhook full payload storage (WebhookCall.requestBody, responseBody) | Debugging necessity vs data minimization | A: Essential for webhook debugging, retry logic, and customer support. Webhooks are system-to-system, not direct user data. <br><br> B: Contains PII (recipient emails, names) and should be redacted or expire after 30 days. Current indefinite retention violates minimization. |
| AI document processing (Google Vertex AI integration) | Service provider vs data processor | A: Google Vertex AI is data processor under GDPR. If DPA in place, acceptable. Users opt-in per team. <br><br> B: Sending document images to third party without explicit per-document consent is high-risk. Users may not understand "AI features enabled" means documents sent to Google. Needs clearer disclosure. |
| Audit log retention (DocumentAuditLog, UserSecurityAuditLog) | Compliance vs privacy | A: Long retention needed for legal compliance, fraud investigation, dispute resolution. Archival after 90 days acceptable. <br><br> B: Indefinite retention with PII (email, name, IP) is excessive. Should anonymize after 1-2 years or delete with user account. Current approach violates storage limitation. |
| Default encryption keys in .env.example | Example vs production | A: .env.example is template, not production config. Developers expected to change keys. Warning commented out in crypto.ts (lines 19-27) but should be uncommented. <br><br> B: Many developers deploy defaults. Keys should be randomly generated on first run, not have defaults. Security-by-obscurity fails at scale. This is critical issue. |

## Red Herrings

Things that look like issues within this skill's focus area but are not.

| Item | Why It Looks Like an Issue | Why It's Not |
|------|--------------------------|-------------|
| `password` field in User and Account tables (schema.prisma lines 44, 310) | Storing passwords appears insecure | Passwords are bcrypt hashed (SALT_ROUNDS = 10, hash.ts line 1-10). Storage of hashes is standard practice. The redundancy during RR7 migration is suboptimal but not a security breach. |
| `signature` field in User table (line 46) storing signature as string | Appears to be storing sensitive biometric signature data | Based on context, this is likely user's name or initials for typed signatures, not biometric image. If image, would be base64 but field is `String?` not `@db.Text`, suggesting short text. |
| `Recipient.token` field (line 585) stored in plaintext | Unencrypted access token looks vulnerable | This is signing token, meant to be in URLs. Must be plaintext to validate. Hashing would prevent lookup. Token is randomly generated and single-use per recipient. Appropriate for purpose. |
| OAuth credentials in environment variables (NEXT_PRIVATE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, etc.) | Secrets in env vars seems insecure | Standard practice for containerized apps. Environment variables are deployment-specific and not committed to git (.env vs .env.example). Acceptable pattern with proper ops security. |
| `documentData` stored as `String` type (DocumentData.data, line 500) | Storing entire document in text field seems inefficient and insecure | Type is `DocumentDataType` enum: BYTES, BYTES_64, or S3_PATH (lines 486-490). S3_PATH stores only key, not content. BYTES_64 is base64 encoded. Structure supports both DB and S3 storage. Design is sound. |
| `WebhookTriggerEvents` enum includes `DOCUMENT_OPENED` (line 170) | Tracking document opens seems privacy-invasive | This is webhook event for sender notification, not analytics tracking. Sender needs to know when recipient opens document for business workflow. Legitimate business purpose. |
| Multiple `createdAt` and `updatedAt` timestamps | Excessive temporal tracking | These are standard audit fields for database records. Required for proper data management, debugging, and legal compliance. Not privacy concern unless logged indefinitely (see audit log findings above). |
| `UserSecurityAuditLogType` enum with granular events (lines 89-108) | Detailed activity tracking seems surveillance-like | This is security audit log for user's own account activity. User can view their own security log. Provides transparency and security (detect unauthorized access). Aligns with PbD Principle 4. |
